role_summary: UCLA student and 1961 Freedom Rider (arrested and severely beaten in Houston, Texas); chairman of the UCLA political group Platform; UCLA representative to the National Student Association (NSA) in 1963; National Civil Rights Director of the National Student Association in the mid-1960s; participant in the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party's Congressional Challenge Strategy Committee; civil-rights program officer at the Ford Foundation (1967-1968); subsequent career as employment-law attorney in Los Angeles.
ai_reading: McNichols's top semantic neighbor is C. T. Vivian (entry 134) at a high cosine count of 201, a strong single-neighbor signature in this catalog. The Vivian pairing is the catalog's clearest cross-region Freedom-Rider-and-direct-action-trainer cosine: Vivian was one of the SCLC field staff who participated in the Freedom Rides and who trained the Nashville-based riders; McNichols was a UCLA-based rider who joined the broader 1961 network. The novel observation his page surfaces is the West-Coast-student-into-national-civil-rights-staff signature: McNichols's UCLA-Platform / NSA / Ford Foundation career arc is the catalog's clearest case of a Freedom-Rider-to-institutional-foundation-staff trajectory that the historiography often subordinates to the more-photographed Mississippi-and-Alabama field-organizer biographies.
biographical_paragraph: While McNichols's LoC oral history carries a not-auditable audit tier (the catalog cannot independently certify the transcript against primary sources for this entry, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external archives before citation), his external biographical record is documented in the Veterans of the Civil Rights Movement archive [src: 1, src: 2]. Steven Everett McNichols was a UCLA undergraduate from New York when he joined the 1961 Freedom Rides; he was arrested at the Houston bus station and severely beaten by a white mob [src: 2]. He returned to UCLA after the Rides and led Platform, the early-1960s UCLA political organization; he was elected UCLA's representative to the National Student Association in 1963 and rose to head the NSA's Civil Rights Division in the mid-1960s. In that role he was a participant in the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party's Congressional Challenge Strategy Committee (the 1964-1965 effort to unseat the all-white Mississippi congressional delegation) [src: 2]. He worked at the Ford Foundation in 1967-1968 as a program officer making grants to civil rights organizations and leaders, earned a Master's in economics at the University of Texas, worked for Los Angeles Mayor Tom Bradley, and built a subsequent career as an attorney specializing in plaintiff's employment law in California [src: 2].

interview_snippets.speaker: Steven McNichols
interview_snippets.lead_in: McNichols describes his routine as a working UCLA student in Los Angeles in the early 1960s.
interview_snippets.quote: I was in L.A., Working and supporting myself and going to UCLA. And every spare moment I had was devoted to the Civil Rights Movement.

interview_snippets.speaker: Steven McNichols
interview_snippets.lead_in: On hearing about the sit-ins and Freedom Rides from New York while at UCLA, and what joining a ride meant to him.
interview_snippets.quote: And it was, you know, to me, it was a chosen thing. I would be lucky if I could be a Freedom Rider. And that's how I felt about it.

interview_snippets.speaker: Steven McNichols
interview_snippets.lead_in: McNichols describes the dress code the group of eighteen demonstrators kept at the Houston Union Station coffee shop in 1961.
interview_snippets.quote: We're all well dressed. It was in the day when everybody who participated in demonstrations. When we're coats and ties.

interview_snippets.speaker: Steven McNichols
interview_snippets.lead_in: After his release on bond, McNichols recounts a local television interview in which the riders said they had been beaten in the Harris County jail. He had been kicked and his hands injured in the beating.
interview_snippets.quote: One TV station came over and interviewed us. I was standing there. My arms crossed tightly wound.

interview_snippets.speaker: Steven McNichols
interview_snippets.lead_in: On meeting Rita Schwerner, widow of murdered Mississippi Freedom Summer worker Michael Schwerner, during the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party Congressional Challenge work.
interview_snippets.quote: one of the people I met there was Rita Schwerner. And Rita and I became quite close. Rita Schwerner, Mickey Schwerner's widow, and we became quite close. And she was a real conscience for me. Not that I didn't have a conscience. But if anything, she accentuated. She was more militant than I was.

interview_snippets.speaker: Rick Tuttle
interview_snippets.lead_in: Rick Tuttle, a fellow UCLA student who later did COFO field work in Mississippi, recalls meeting McNichols through the National Student Association chapter.
interview_snippets.quote: And I then met Steve McNichols. And Steve McNichols, who later that year in 1963, became the NSA rep, was active in NSA, and it turned out, and they thought of me as a fellow NSA, having had this background, and so on.

interview_snippets.speaker: Rick Tuttle
interview_snippets.lead_in: Tuttle places McNichols's West Coast ride within the larger 1961 Freedom Rides strategy.
interview_snippets.quote: But Steve had done the freedom rides, in fact, from the West Coast. Part of the idea on the freedom rides was to try to fill the jails and to come in a pincer movement, West Coast, East Coast.

gallery.alt: Birmingham News front page coverage of the May 14, 1961 Anniston Freedom Rider bus attack
gallery.caption: Theodore Gaffney's photograph of the Anniston Greyhound bus attack on the front page of the Birmingham News, Sunday May 14, 1961. The bus had been firebombed by a Ku Klux Klan mob on U.S. Highway 78 outside Anniston. McNichols joined the 1961 Freedom Rides later that summer on the Los Angeles CORE ride to Houston, Texas, where he was arrested at the Union Station coffee shop and beaten in the Harris County jail.
